quiz_questions.question_text: What is acceleration?

options.text: The speed of an object at one instant
The rate of change of distance
The rate of change of velocity with respect to time
The total displacement of an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration a = Δv/Δt, measuring how quickly velocity changes. Its SI unit is m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of acceleration?

options.text: Newtons (N)
Meters per second squared (m/s²)
Meters per second (m/s)
Kilometers per hour (km/h)

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration has units of velocity divided by time: (m/s)/s = m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: If a car increases its velocity from 0 to 20 m/s in 5 seconds, what is its acceleration?

options.text: 100 m/s²
4 m/s²
20 m/s²

quiz_questions.question_text: Can an object accelerate while maintaining constant speed?

options.text: Only if the object has zero mass
Only in outer space
No — constant speed always means constant velocity
Yes — if the direction of motion changes (e.g., circular motion), velocity changes even at constant speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is a change in velocity (a vector). Since velocity has both magnitude and direction, changing direction at constant speed still constitutes acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is deceleration?

options.text: Acceleration in the opposite direction to the velocity, causing the object to slow down
Negative acceleration only
Zero acceleration
Acceleration that equals exactly −9.8 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: Deceleration occurs when the acceleration vector opposes the velocity vector, reducing speed. It is not simply 'negative acceleration' — the sign depends on the chosen coordinate system.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object has a positive velocity and a negative acceleration. What is happening?

options.text: The object is moving backward
The object is speeding up in the negative direction
The object is stationary
The object is slowing down — the acceleration opposes the direction of motion

quiz_questions.explanation: When acceleration is opposite to velocity, the speed decreases. A positive velocity with negative acceleration means the object moves in the positive direction but is decelerating.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?

options.text: Acceleration
Displacement
Velocity
Speed

quiz_questions.explanation: On a v-t graph, slope = Δv/Δt = acceleration. A steeper slope means greater acceleration. A horizontal line means constant velocity (zero acceleration).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a horizontal line on a velocity-time graph mean?

options.text: The object is stationary
The object is accelerating at a constant rate
The object is decelerating
The object is moving at constant velocity (zero acceleration)

quiz_questions.explanation: A horizontal line means velocity is not changing with time, so acceleration is zero. The object moves at constant velocity (uniform motion).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?

options.text: Force
Acceleration
Displacement
Speed

quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a v-t graph equals displacement: ∫v dt = Δx. This works for any shape of v-t graph.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object has zero velocity but nonzero acceleration. Is this possible?

options.text: Only if the object has infinite mass
Yes — for example, a ball thrown upward has zero velocity at its highest point but still has gravitational acceleration downward
No — zero velocity means zero acceleration
Only in a vacuum

quiz_questions.explanation: At the peak of a vertical throw, v = 0 momentarily but a = −g = −9.8 m/s² (gravity never stops). This is why the ball immediately begins falling back down.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes acceleration?

options.text: The total change in position of an object
How fast an object is moving at one instant
The rate at which velocity changes with respect to time — a vector quantity with SI unit m/s²
The rate at which distance changes with time

quiz_questions.explanation: a = Δv/Δt (or dv/dt). Acceleration is a vector, having both magnitude and direction. It tells us how quickly and in what direction the velocity is changing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes constant acceleration?

options.text: Any acceleration that is positive
Acceleration that increases over time
Acceleration that is always 9.8 m/s²
Acceleration that has the same magnitude and direction at every instant, producing a uniformly changing velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Under constant acceleration, velocity changes by the same amount each second. This produces a straight line on a v-t graph and a parabola on a position-time graph.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes instantaneous acceleration?

options.text: The maximum acceleration experienced
The acceleration of an object at a specific instant, equal to the derivative of velocity with respect to time (dv/dt)
Acceleration measured with a stopwatch
The average acceleration over the entire trip

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous acceleration is the limit of Δv/Δt as Δt → 0. It equals the slope of the velocity-time graph at that instant and can differ from the average acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes free fall?

options.text: Motion of an object under the influence of gravity alone, with no air resistance, where all objects accelerate at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² regardless of mass
Falling from a very high altitude
Motion without any forces acting
Any downward motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Free fall occurs when gravity is the only force. All objects — regardless of mass, shape, or composition — experience the same acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward (ignoring air resistance).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a velocity-time graph?

options.text: A graph plotting velocity on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis, where slope equals acceleration and area equals displacement
A graph that only works for constant velocity
A distance-speed relationship diagram
A graph showing position changes over time

quiz_questions.explanation: V-t graphs encode motion completely: slope gives acceleration, area under the curve gives displacement, and horizontal intercepts show when the object reverses direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes uniform acceleration?

options.text: Motion at a constant speed
Motion with continuously increasing acceleration
Motion in a straight line at any speed
Motion in which velocity changes by equal amounts in equal time intervals — the acceleration remains constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform acceleration means a = constant. Velocity increases (or decreases) linearly with time: v = v₀ + at. This is the simplest type of accelerated motion and is described by the SUVAT equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes non-uniform acceleration?

options.text: Acceleration that changes in magnitude and/or direction over time, making velocity change at a varying rate
Acceleration that is always negative
Very large constant acceleration
Acceleration in curved motion only

quiz_questions.explanation: Non-uniform acceleration means a ≠ constant. Examples include a car in traffic (varying throttle) and SHM (acceleration proportional to displacement). Calculus (derivatives/integrals) is needed to analyze such motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the acceleration due to gravity (g)?

options.text: The speed limit for falling objects
The acceleration experienced by objects in free fall near Earth's surface, approximately 9.8 m/s² directed downward
The force of gravity on an object
The weight of an object divided by its volume

quiz_questions.explanation: g ≈ 9.81 m/s² at Earth's surface. It varies slightly with altitude and latitude. On the Moon, g ≈ 1.6 m/s². It is the same for all objects regardless of mass (in the absence of air resistance).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes jerk?

options.text: The maximum acceleration in a system
A sudden stop
An error in acceleration measurement
The rate of change of acceleration with respect to time (da/dt), with SI units of m/s³

quiz_questions.explanation: Jerk = da/dt = d³x/dt³. It describes how smoothly acceleration changes. High jerk means abrupt changes in acceleration, which feels uncomfortable in vehicles and can damage machinery.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes terminal velocity?

options.text: The constant maximum velocity reached by a falling object when air resistance equals gravitational force, resulting in zero net force and zero acceleration
The fastest possible speed in the universe
The velocity at which an object hits the ground
The velocity at which an object leaves Earth's atmosphere

quiz_questions.explanation: Terminal velocity occurs when drag force balances weight: mg = F_drag. At this point a = 0 and velocity remains constant. A skydiver's terminal velocity is about 55 m/s (200 km/h) in a spread position.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car accelerates from rest at 3 m/s² for 10 seconds. How far does it travel and what is its final speed?

options.text: Final speed = 30 m/s, distance = 300 m
Final speed = 30 m/s, distance = 30 m
Final speed = 10 m/s, distance = 150 m
Final speed = 30 m/s, distance = 150 m — using v = at = 3(10) = 30 m/s and s = ½at² = ½(3)(100) = 150 m

quiz_questions.explanation: v = u + at = 0 + 3(10) = 30 m/s. s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½(3)(10²) = 150 m. The car reaches 30 m/s (108 km/h) having traveled 150 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball is thrown straight up at 20 m/s. Ignoring air resistance, how high does it go before momentarily stopping?

options.text: Approximately 20.4 m — using v² = u² + 2as: 0 = 20² + 2(−9.8)s, s = 400/19.6 ≈ 20.4 m

quiz_questions.explanation: At the peak v = 0. Using v² = u² + 2as with a = −9.8 m/s²: 0 = 400 − 19.6s → s = 400/19.6 ≈ 20.4 m. Gravity decelerates the ball at 9.8 m/s² until it stops.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bicycle goes from 5 m/s to 15 m/s in 4 seconds. Then from 15 m/s to 35 m/s in 4 seconds. Is the acceleration constant?

options.text: Cannot tell without position data
Yes — the cyclist is always speeding up
Yes — both intervals are 4 seconds long
No — the first interval has a = (15−5)/4 = 2.5 m/s² and the second has a = (35−15)/4 = 5 m/s², showing non-uniform acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: First interval: a₁ = 10/4 = 2.5 m/s². Second interval: a₂ = 20/4 = 5.0 m/s². Since 2.5 ≠ 5.0, the acceleration is not constant — it increased, indicating non-uniform acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two balls are dropped from the same height. Ball A has mass 1 kg and Ball B has mass 5 kg. Ignoring air resistance, which hits the ground first?

options.text: They hit at the same time — in free fall, all objects accelerate at g regardless of mass
It depends on the height
Ball B hits first because it is heavier
Ball A hits first because it is lighter

quiz_questions.explanation: Galileo's principle: in the absence of air resistance, all objects fall with the same acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s². Mass does not affect free-fall acceleration. Both balls experience identical motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car traveling at 30 m/s applies brakes and decelerates at 5 m/s². How far does it travel before stopping?

options.text: 6 m

quiz_questions.explanation: v² = u² + 2as. With v = 0, u = 30, a = −5: 0 = 900 − 10s → s = 90 m. Alternatively, average velocity = (30 + 0)/2 = 15 m/s, time = 30/5 = 6 s, distance = 15 × 6 = 90 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rocket accelerates upward at 20 m/s². If the rocket starts from rest, what is its velocity after 5 seconds?

options.text: 4 m/s upward
100 m/s downward
25 m/s upward
100 m/s upward — v = u + at = 0 + 20(5) = 100 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: v = u + at = 0 + 20 × 5 = 100 m/s upward. The rocket gains 20 m/s of velocity every second it accelerates.

quiz_questions.question_text: A skydiver jumps from a plane and initially accelerates at 9.8 m/s². After some time, she reaches a terminal velocity of 55 m/s. What happened to her acceleration?

options.text: She experienced negative acceleration the whole time
Her acceleration stayed at 9.8 m/s² the entire time
Her acceleration decreased from 9.8 m/s² to 0 as air resistance increased to match her weight — at terminal velocity, net force is zero so acceleration is zero
Her acceleration increased beyond 9.8 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: Initially, air resistance is small and acceleration ≈ g = 9.8 m/s². As speed increases, air resistance grows. When air resistance equals weight, net force = 0, acceleration = 0, and velocity stays constant at terminal velocity (55 m/s).

quiz_questions.question_text: A train's velocity-time graph shows a straight line from (0, 0) to (10, 20). What is the train's acceleration and displacement during this interval?

options.text: Acceleration = 2 m/s², Displacement = 200 m
Acceleration = 10 m/s², Displacement = 100 m
Acceleration = 20 m/s², Displacement = 200 m
Acceleration = 2 m/s² (slope of the line); Displacement = 100 m (area of triangle: ½ × 10 × 20)

quiz_questions.explanation: Slope = Δv/Δt = 20/10 = 2 m/s². Area = ½ × base × height = ½ × 10 × 20 = 100 m. This confirms s = ½at² = ½(2)(100) = 100 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A elevator accelerates upward at 2 m/s² for 3 seconds, then moves at constant velocity for 5 seconds, then decelerates at 2 m/s² for 3 seconds. What is the total displacement?

options.text: 30 m — only the constant velocity phase counts
48 m — 9 m accelerating (½ × 2 × 9), 30 m constant (6 × 5), 9 m decelerating (½ × 2 × 9 or equivalently 6×3 − ½×2×9)

33 m — Phase 1: s₁ = ½(2)(9) = 9 m (reaches 6 m/s); Phase 2: s₂ = 6(5) = 30 m; Phase 3: s₃ = 6(3) − ½(2)(9) = 18 − 9 = 9 m (but it's only decelerating so it's ½(6)(3) = 9 m) — wait, let me recalculate correctly. Total = 9 + 30 + 9 = 48 m
9 m — only the first phase matters

quiz_questions.explanation: Phase 1: v = 0 + 2(3) = 6 m/s. s₁ = ½(2)(3²) = 9 m. Phase 2: constant 6 m/s for 5 s. s₂ = 30 m. Phase 3: s₃ = 6(3) − ½(2)(3²) = 18 − 9 = 9 m. Total: 9 + 30 + 9 = 48 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: An athlete practices sprints. Run 1: 0 to 8 m/s in 2 seconds. Run 2: 0 to 8 m/s in 4 seconds. Both reach the same final speed. What is different?

options.text: Run 1 has more displacement because it's faster
Nothing is different — same initial and final speed means same motion
Run 2 covers more distance because it takes longer
The acceleration is different — Run 1 has a = 4 m/s² while Run 2 has a = 2 m/s². Run 1 required double the acceleration despite reaching the same speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Run 1: a = 8/2 = 4 m/s², s = ½(4)(4) = 8 m. Run 2: a = 8/4 = 2 m/s², s = ½(2)(16) = 16 m. Despite the same final speed, the acceleration differs and Run 2 covers more distance because it takes longer to reach the same speed.
